articles.title: Feathered Punk Rockers Win Big Photo Prize!
articles.summary: What has spiky feathers, loves to squabble, and just won a huge photo prize? Three baby egrets that look like tiny punk rockers! The picture, called 'The Punk Rockers,' was taken by Jacqueline Burke, a veterinarian and photographer, at a bird breeding ground in New Jersey. The babies were no bigger than a golf ball when curled up, and they spent all their time pecking and fighting while a long-legged parent watched nearby. Burke's photo won the grand prize in the Smithsonian Magazine's 23rd annual contest, beating more than 17,000 entries from 108 countries. Other winners include a barefoot woman at a Hindu festival and a colorful chameleon. Burke used to watch birds with binoculars but switched to a camera…

articles.title: London Exhibition Tells Stories of Southern Lebanon's People
articles.summary: Have you ever felt like nobody is listening to your story? That's how many people in southern Lebanon feel. But a new exhibition in London called 'Forget Me Not' is making sure their voices are heard. The show features videos, photos, and drawings that tell how people have survived wars and difficult times. One video shares a powerful message: 'We are attached to this land just like a tree is rooted in its soil.' The exhibition was created by Rasha Kotaiche and Ali Abou Khalil, who have family roots in the region. They wanted to share real stories—not just what you see on the news. You'll see drawings by Lebanese schoolchildren celebrating independence day, plus photos of pomegranates and oranges.…

articles.title: Art World Olympics: What's Cool at the 2026 Venice Biennale
articles.summary: Imagine the Olympics, but for art! That's the Venice Biennale, a giant art show in Venice, Italy, happening every two years. This year's main exhibition, 'In Minor Keys,' was planned by Koyo Kouoh, the first African woman to be the main curator. Sadly, she passed away, but her team made sure her vision came to life. There are 110 artists showing work, including Alma Allen at the U.S. pavilion. But there's drama: judges won't give awards to countries whose leaders are charged with serious crimes by the International Criminal Court. Also, Qatar is building a brand-new pavilion—the first new one in 30 years! And for the first time, countries like Vietnam, Somalia, and Guinea are joining. There's even an artwork…
